title: Fallen stock and disposal options in the UK
subtitle: What to do with an animal that has died on the holding.
excerpt: An animal that dies on your holding is fallen stock. UK law requires it to be disposed of by an approved route within a specific time. Burial is not permitted for most species. The approved routes are collection by a licensed knacker or renderer, a fallen stock service, or incineration at an approved facility.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Animal by-products regulations: fallen stock disposal guidance, gov.uk. APHA Animal By-Products (ABP) categorisation. National Fallen Stock Company (NFSCo) service guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: Any livestock or poultry that has died on the holding from disease or other causes, rather than being slaughtered for food.
Carcasses, manure, and other materials of animal origin not intended for human consumption. Classified into three categories by UK/EU ABP regulations, each with specific disposal requirements.

content.text: must be disposed of through one of the routes approved under the UK

regulations. Leaving a carcass in a field, burying it without authorisation, or burning it in an open fire is illegal and subject to enforcement. The rules apply regardless of the size of the holding or the type of animal.

An animal that dies must be collected or disposed of by an approved route without delay. In practice that means within forty-eight hours in warm weather, and longer in winter. You may not move a dead animal off your holding to dispose of it yourself, unless you are taking it directly to an approved facility.

The National Fallen Stock Company (NFSCo) scheme provides collection of fallen farm animals for a fee. Registration is free; you pay per collection, per weight band. They collect most livestock species, including horses and cattle. Contact details are at nfscotland.co.uk or nfsco.co.uk for England and Wales.
NFSCo is not available everywhere. In areas not covered, licensed knacker yards or rendering plants provide the same service. Your vet will know which services operate in your area.

Poultry and pet rabbits under 5 kg are exempt from the collection requirement. They may be buried on the holding or incinerated in a suitable incinerator. Deep burial means at least 1 metre below the surface, away from watercourses, and not in a wet area.

A standard garden bonfire is not an approved incineration method for poultry. An approved incinerator (a unit with a secondary burn chamber and a flue) is required. Some small abbatoirs and rendering plants will accept poultry carcasses for a fee.

If an animal dies from suspected notifiable disease, call APHA before calling the fallen stock service. APHA will direct the disposal and may require the carcass to be left in place for investigation. Moving it without authorisation in this situation is an offence.

Keep the carcass in a cool shaded place, away from other stock, until collection. Cover it with a tarpaulin to reduce fly access and prevent disturbance by other animals. Do not put the carcass in a wheelie bin or domestic waste stream.
